data.title: Insurance stocks Lemonade, Mercury among biggest financial gainers of the week
data.description:
Most of the financial stocks making the biggest gains this week were insurance stocks, while most of the biggest decliners inhabit the crypto space.

In the week ended May 30, 2025, the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) rose 1.8%, as the S&P 500 climbed 1.9%. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slid 2.6%, retreating from its record high of almost $112K on May 22.

The biggest gainer of the week, Lemonade (LMND), jumped 8.5%, followed by Mercury General (MCY), which grew 7.6%.

KB Financial (KB), which provides banking, financial services and insurance products in South Korea and other international markets, increased 7.2%.

Pet insurer Trupanion (TRUP) gained 6.8%, while Palomar Holdings (PLMR), another insurance firm, rose 6.6%.

The five financial stocks that fell the most were led by AIFU (AIFU), which distributes insurance products in China, which sank 30% during the week.

Digital assets firm Galaxy Digital (GLXY), which priced an upsized stock offering this week, dropped 26%.

Another crypto company, CleanSpark (CLSK) slid 13%.

eToro Group (ETOR), which started trading publicly on May 14, dipped 11%.

MARA Holdings (MARA), a bitcoin miner and data center company, dropped 9.8% for the week.

data.title: Adani Energy is said to plan $502M share sale to institutional investors
data.description:
Adani Energy Solutions, a firm under Indian billionaire Gautam Adani’s conglomerate, approved plans to raise 43 billion rupees (approximately $502 million) by selling equity to institutional investors.

According to a regulatory filing on Saturday, the company’s board authorized the capital raise through a Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP), which may be executed in multiple phases.

The Adani Group, whose business interests range from infrastructure and logistics to renewable power, has worked to rebuild market confidence following U.S. legal action against its founder.

In April, the group secured $750 million to fund an acquisition, with asset management giant BlackRock (BLK) subscribing to roughly a third of that bond issuance. More recently, its ports division obtained a $150 million loan from Singapore’s DBS Group.

Credit outlooks have also started to stabilize. Fitch Ratings in March removed Adani Energy from its watchlist and assigned a negative outlook, citing improved liquidity access. The Adani group demonstrated adequate funding access following the U.S. bribery allegations, Fitch said.

data.title: Pfizer/Arvinas to seek FDA nod for jointly developed cancer therapy in H2
data.description:
Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) and Arvinas (NASDAQ:ARVN) announced Saturday their plans to submit a new drug application with the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in H2 2025 targeting the U.S. approval of their jointly developed breast cancer therapy, vepdegestrant.

The decision comes despite a mixed outcome for the oral therapy in a Phase 3 trial involving adults with estrogen receptor-positive, human epidermal growth factor receptor 2-negative (ER+/HER2-) advanced or metastatic breast cancer (MBC).

Citing an intent-to-treat (ITT) analysis, the gold standard for assessing controlled trials, Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) and Arvinas (NASDAQ:ARVN) announced in March that vepdegestrant failed to show statistical significance for progression-free survival, the primary goal in their VERITAC-2 global trial.

However, the 624-subject trial reached the same goal for a subset of patients with estrogen receptor 1 (ESR1) mutations. The companies announced detailed results from VERITAC-2 on Saturday ahead of a presentation at the 2025 ASCO Annual Meeting in Chicago, IL.

According to the readout, vepdegestrant as a second-line option indicated a median progression-free survival of five months in ESR1-mutant patients, compared to 2.1 months for those who received the established breast cancer therapy fulvestrant.

In the ITT population, vepdegestrant was linked to a median PFS of 3.7 months compared to 3.6 months for fulvestrant, while the result lacked any statistical significance.

The companies added that the experimental therapy was well tolerated in VERITAC-2, while the rate of discontinuations related to treatment-emergent adverse events stood at 2.9% and 0.7% in the vepdegestrant and fulvestrant groups, respectively.

“We are excited to engage with regulatory authorities on next steps to potentially bring vepdegestrant to healthcare providers and their patients as swiftly as possible,” Arvinas (NASDAQ:ARVN) CEO John Houston remarked.

data.title: Trump’s Tariffs: Key announcements from April and May
data.description:
Ever since U.S. President Donald Trump has taken office, one of the many things that constantly dominated headlines was his tariff policy.

Although Trump had imposed tariffs during his first term, his latest actions roiled markets, disrupted diplomatic ties and shook industries. The back-and-forth tariff stance and retaliatory measures has put investors on shaky ground.

Click here to see the tariff timeline from January this year. Here are the key developments that took place in April and May:

April 2, 2025: President Donald Trump detailed the long-waited tariffs that the U.S. is enacting on imports- a day he has dubbed "Liberation Day." 

The U.S. slapped a tariff of 34% on imports from China, on top of the previously announced 20% tariff on Chinese goods, bringing the total tariff rate to 54%.  The highest rate of 49% was imposed on Cambodia, followed by 48% on Laos.

April 7, 2025: Trump said he'll heap an additional 50% tariff onto Chinese goods entering the U.S. in response to China's 34% retaliatory tariffs on U.S. goods.

April 9, 2025: In response to the U.S.'s 104% tariffs on Chinese goods, Beijing will raise its tariff on U.S. goods to 84%. Trump said he was issuing a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries that had reached out for negotiations. Meanwhile, he slapped China with a higher rate to 125%.

April 11, 2025: China has announced an increase in tariffs on U.S. goods to 125%, up from the previous rate of 84%, and stated it will disregard any additional tariff hikes moving forward.

April 12, 2025: Trump's administration exempted smartphones, computers and other electronics mostly imported from China from reciprocal tariffs, providing major relief for American tech players like Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA) and Dell (DELL).

April 14, 2025: President Trump reiterated his plans to impose levies in the “not too distant future” against pharmaceutical goods.

April 15, 2025: Trump has floated a potential reprieve for the 25% tariffs on automobile and auto part imports.

April 16, 2025: The White House clarified that a max China tariff rate of 245% included in a recent fact sheet referred to some goods and combined new fentanyl and reciprocal tariffs with existing 301 tariffs from the previous two administrations.

April 22, 2025: The United States imposed duties, up to 3,521%, on solar imports from Cambodia, Vietnam, Malaysia, and Thailand.

April 23, 2025: President Trump has said tariffs on China won't be as high as 145%, but it won’t be zero.

April 24, 2025: China called on the United States to lift all unilateral tariffs and stated that no trade deal negotiations were currently underway.

May 5, 2025: Trump announced a sweeping 100% tariff on all movies produced outside the U.S., citing the need to protect a "dying" American film industry.

May 9, 2025: Trump suggested that reducing the current 145% tariff on China to 80% "seems right."

May 12, 2025: The U.S. and China have agreed to reduce tariffs on each other’s goods for 90 days. Each country will cut tariffs by 115%, with the U.S. cutting tariffs on Chinese imports from 145% to 30%, and China cutting tariffs on U.S. imports from 125% to 10%. 

May 13, 2025: India has proposed tariffs on select U.S. goods in retaliation for Washington’s steel and aluminum duties amid trade talks. Meanwhile, Trump said China has agreed to remove non-tariff barriers to U.S. imports as part of a new trade agreement reached. He also said that the U.S. will reduce the "de minimis" tariff on shipments from China to 54% from the previous rate of 120%.

May 23, 2025: Tariffs on all smartphones not built in the U.S., notably those from Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), are coming by the end of June, Trump said. He also threatened a 50% tariff on the EU starting June 1.

May 25, 2025: Trump said he has decided to hold off on imposing 50% tariffs on the EU until July 9 to allow negotiations to continue.

May 28, 2025: A federal trade court has struck down the "reciprocal" tariffs that President Trump imposed on what he called "Liberation Day," putting an immediate stop to the collection of a sweeping set of levies imposed by him.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: S&P 500 looks expensive, but policy winners may be mispriced: BofA
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) stock index may look pricey at the surface, but beneath the index-level froth, some policy-linked investment themes are still trading at discounts, said Savita Subramanian, equity and quant strategist at Bank of America.

In a research note dated May 29, Subramanian said the S&P 500 (SP500) is now valued above its historical averages on all 20 measures tracked by the bank. Even when adjusted for equal weighting -- dampening the impact of mega-cap names -- the index remains elevated, with the median stock now 10% more expensive than average.

Yet policy-sensitive segments of the market reveal less efficiency, Subramanian said. In particular, she sees opportunity in inflation hedges, rate-sensitive names and overlooked beneficiaries of recent fiscal legislation.

‘Buy oil, not gold’

Among the clearest conclusions from BofA’s analysis is a preference for crude oil over gold as an inflation hedge. While both commodities have historically performed well in inflationary periods, oil now trades at one of its most attractive relative value entry points since the Covid-19 crash and the early 2000s tech bubble. By contrast, gold's relative multiple has normalized after years of overvaluation.

Tariff trades are priced in, but not domestic policy winners

Companies with primarily domestic revenue streams re-rated sharply following “Liberation Day,” BofA notes, trading at up to a 15% premium versus their typical 5% discount under the prior Trump administration. Although some of this premium has faded, domestic-facing stocks remain relatively rich.

Domestic-facing stocks are companies that don’t have any overseas sales, while foreign-exposed stocks are in the top 10% of companies with sales in other countries, according to BofA’s screens.

Trump's "Liberation Day" refers to a major policy announcement on April 2, when he declared a sweeping set of tariffs on imports from key U.S. trade partners. The move introduced uncertainty into global markets and trade negotiations, as investors sought clarity on U.S. trade policy.

While some domestic-facing stocks are pricey, others aligned with various pillars of Trump’s “Big Beautiful Bill” for U.S. spending have underwhelmed, according to BofA. Across every basket constructed to reflect the bill’s goals (including tax cuts, infrastructure and manufacturing incentives), performance has lagged peers, and valuations remain below historical norms, the bank said.

Tax relief and ‘Made in America’ plays still on sale

Despite promises of higher take-home pay for consumers, stocks sensitive to disposable income growth are trading below their average multiples from the Trump 1.0 to Trump 2.0 period. Similarly, domestic manufacturers and firms benefiting from R&D and capital expenditure incentives under the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TJCA) have failed to re-rate. These companies remain cheap on a historical basis and have yet to reflect anticipated policy tailwinds.

BofA sees the most opportunity in industries including metals and mining, media, biotechnology and personal care products.

The bank said “value traps” are most likely to be found in auto parts, hotel and resorts REITs, air freight and logistics, household durables and passenger airlines.

Typically, a value trap is a stock that appears cheap based on traditional valuation metrics, such as low price-to-earnings or price-to-book ratios, but is actually a poor investment because its fundamentals are deteriorating. Investors may be lured in by the apparent bargain, only to see the stock continue to underperform due to structural issues, declining earnings or poor management.

Hawks not yet rewarded

While rising deficits tied to recent legislation and tariff-related costs could eventually push long-term rates higher, the market has yet to price in the risk. Subramanian points out that stocks typically benefiting from higher interest rates are still trading in line with long-term averages, suggesting room for catch-up if bond yields begin to reflect sovereign risk.

Opportunity in the overlooked

With the index broadly expensive, BofA recommends investors look beyond the headline S&P 500 (SP500) multiples.

“Under the surface of the S&P 500 and relative to other asset classes, there have been big moves that are worth a look, especially with respect to policy change coming fast in the last several months,” Subramanian said.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Southwest Airlines, Regeneron and Nvidia among top picks
data.description:
The S&P500 (SP500)closed in the red, after the week witnessed muted inflation data, a court striking down President Trump's April tariffs and earnings reports from firms including Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Costco (COST).

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) declined 0.12%, while Dow (DJI) fell 0.3%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Deutsche Bank gives bullish rating to Southwest Airlines

Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) was upgraded by Deutsche Bank to Buy from Hold, with analyst Michael Linenberg pointing to its refreshed board of directors and Elliott Management as having ushered in a new era of change at the company, which the firm thinks will drive higher shareholder returns.

Buybacks were also highlighted as a potential tailwind for shares. The brokerage also added that Southwest's return on invested capital will significantly improve over the next two years.

Regeneron downgraded on COPD trial data

Wells Fargo downgraded Regeneron (NASDAQ:REGN) to Equal Weight from Overweight, as a Phase 3 trial failure for its antibody therapy itepekimab, developed with Sanofi for a lung disorder known as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).

“We think itepekimab’s disappointing data create a big challenge for REGN in the long term,” Wells Fargo analyst Mohit Bansal wrote.

Bansal, who kept a PT of $580 per share, said investors are less excited about the rest of the pipeline.

Salesforce downgraded by RBC on Informatica deal

RBC Capital downgraded Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) to Sector Perform from Outperform and set a $275 PT, citing “execution risk” tied to the planned acquisition of Informatica.

Analyst Rishi Jaluria flagged concerns over the integration of past deals and warned the move could distract from Salesforce’s core operations.

Meanwhile, most analysts were impressed after the company surpassed estimates in its first quarter financial results and guidance. Evercore and Morgan Stanley reiterated their Outperform rating, while Wells Fargo maintained its Equal-weight rating.

BofA cuts rating on SentinelOne after Q1 results

BofA downgraded SentinelOne (NYSE:S) to Neutral from Buy, and cut PT to $21 from $24, after the cybersecurity company reported mixed first-quarter results and guidance.

“The focal point of the last two quarters has been weaker guidance, with growth of 21%, -500bps off Street’s initial FY26 expectations,” Bank of America analyst Tal Liani said, adding that the brokerage sees weaker business trends.

Liani said while the endpoint market is one of the most attractive in cybersecurity, SentinelOne is already benefiting from it, and therefore he believes “the valuation is already reflective of the risks, yet the stock lacks upside catalysts,”

Wall Street praises Nvidia after strong quarter

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) was in focus after the AI-giant’s stronger-than-expected results and guidance impressed investors and analysts.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives reiterated his Outperform rating and described the quarterly results as “robust” and the commentary around demand as “very positive,” even when taking China into account.

Morgan Stanley analyst Joseph Moore kept his Overweight rating and upped PT by $10 to $170, while BofA analyst Vivek Arya hiked PT to $180 from $160. Jefferies analyst Blayne Curtis noted that the second-half of the year is “shaping up nicely” for Nvidia, with “likely beat/raises from here.”

Leidos Holdings (NYSE:LDOS) was downgraded to Neutral from Outperform by Baird, citing a tougher outlook for government services providers and a softening bookings environment. Analyst Peter Arment expressed caution regarding the sector's near-term trajectory, warning that headline risks are likely to weigh on sentiment in the coming months.

Wells Fargo upgraded HCA Healthcare (NYSE:HCA) and Tenet Healthcare (NYSE:THC), noting that policy risks related to the hospital sector are receding with the House passage of budget reconciliation legislation.

Deutsche Bank resumed coverage on Ralph Lauren (NYSE:RL) with a Buy in a note titled "Not Your Grandpa's Polo." Analyst Krisztina Katai highlighted that few companies within the DB's coverage fit the description of high-quality with good underlying fundamentals, pricing power, white space opportunity, and limited China sourcing, but the firm thinks Ralph Lauren checks all the boxes.

data.title: Gilead’s Trodelvy with Merck’s Keytruda cuts disease progression/ death in breast cancer
data.description:
Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ:GILD) announced Saturday that its antibody-drug conjugate Trodelvy, in combination with Merck’s (NYSE:MRK) immunotherapy Keytruda, cut the risk of disease progression or death by 35% as a first-line option for an aggressive type of breast cancer.

Citing data from its ASCENT-04 pivotal Phase 3 trial, the Foster City, California-based biotech said that patients on Trodelvy + Keytruda indicated a median progression-free survival of 11.2 months compared to 7.8 months for Keytruda plus chemotherapy.

Data at a median follow-up of 14 months further indicated a 35% reduction in the risk of disease progression or death with a highly statistically significant and clinically meaningful effect, the company added.

The global trial enrolled more than 400 individuals with newly diagnosed locally advanced or metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) whose cancers expressed the PD-L1 tumor biomarker. Considered the most aggressive type of breast cancer, TNBC is estimated to make up nearly 15% of all breast cancer cases.

Gilead (NASDAQ:GILD) added that ASCENT-04 would proceed further to evaluate data for overall survival (OS), which, being the study’s secondary endpoint, indicated a positive trend at the time of the analysis.  

In terms of tolerability, the company said there were no new safety signals, and Trodelvy plus Keytruda demonstrated a safety profile in line with the established safety profile of individual drugs. However, 12% in the Trodelvy plus Keytruda arm and 31% in the Keytruda plus chemotherapy arm discontinued the trial due to adverse events.

The ASCENT-04 data will be part of a presentation at the ongoing 2025 ASCO Annual Meeting in Chicago, IL.

data.title: OPEC+ moves ahead with 3rd output increase amid Russian doubts
data.description:
In a bold move that underscores a major strategic shift, OPEC+ agreed to ramp up oil production for a third consecutive month, defying objections from several member nations, including Russia.

The decision reflects a growing determination by the alliance, led by Saudi Arabia, to increase supply even as global crude prices face mounting pressure.

The group announced on Saturday that it will add 411,000 barrels a day to the market in July, mirroring output hikes already planned for May and June. This increase marks a stark departure from the bloc’s traditional approach of propping up prices and suggests a more aggressive market-share strategy.

Analysts cited by Bloomberg News point to multiple motives behind Riyadh’s push: penalizing nations that have consistently exceeded production quotas, clawing back ground lost to U.S. shale producers and aligning with Washington’s long-standing preference for lower energy costs.

While the additional barrels may offer some relief to consumers and policymakers battling inflation, they also carry the risk of extending the current slump in oil prices, which poses economic challenges for many producers.

Internal tensions flared during the virtual meeting, as Russia, Algeria and Oman were said to urge a more cautious pace of increases, Bloomberg News reported, citing delegates that asked not to be named.

The divergent views between Saudi Arabia and Russia, two of the group’s powerhouses, are expected to resurface when the alliance reconvenes on July 6 to set August production targets.

Mounting price pressure

OPEC+’s recent production spree already has weighed heavily on crude markets. In April, the Brent global benchmark briefly slid below $60 a barrel, the lowest in four years, after the group announced it would triple planned output increases, compounding market weakness from softening demand and global trade uncertainties.

Although prices have since rebounded slightly, with Brent crude hovering near $64 per barrel, Saudi Arabia still faces significant fiscal strain. According to estimates from the International Monetary Fund, the kingdom needs prices above $90 to fund Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s ambitious spending agenda. Budget shortfalls have already forced cuts to marquee projects like the Neom megacity.

Some market observers cited by Bloomberg News said the July agreement may offer mild reassurance, as rumors had suggested an even larger production hike was on the table.

However, efforts to enforce discipline within the cartel appear ineffective. Kazakhstan, a frequent violator of its quota, continues to overproduce and has signaled no intention of complying. Energy Minister Yerlan Akkenzhenov said Thursday that the government lacks the authority to curb output from both state-owned operations and international partners.

Meanwhile, in the United States, shale producers are feeling the squeeze. Companies such as Diamondback Energy (FANG) have acknowledged that their output may have already peaked, despite political pressure to ramp up drilling activity.

Summer demand outlook

With the July increase, OPEC+ will have restored more than half of the 2.2 million barrels a day it previously held back from the market, a process originally slated to continue through 2026. Decisions on further supply restoration are expected in the months ahead.

For some analysts, the timing makes sense. As summer travel drives up gasoline use in the U.S. and domestic consumption spikes across the Middle East due to seasonal air conditioning demand, the additional oil may help meet short-term needs.

“Fundamentals in the right-here, right-now are strong — inventories are very low,” Amrita Sen, director of research at consultant Energy Aspects, said to Bloomberg television before the meeting. “It is a good time for OPEC+ to add barrels to the market, so I don’t see why they wouldn’t.”

Still, not everyone is optimistic. J.P. Morgan projects that Brent could dip into the high $50s later this year, with rising output from OPEC+ contributing to a surplus exceeding 2 million barrels a day globally, Bloomberg News reported.

data.title: Hims & Hers Health is reportedly laying off staff
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) is laying off about 4% of its employees as the telehealth firm adapts its strategy in a market where the sale of cheaper compounded versions of popular weight loss drugs is no longer allowed, Bloomberg News reported.

The decision is set to affect the jobs of 68 people employed across various departments of the San Francisco-based health tech, which employs over 1,600 employees nationwide.

While Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) declined to say which positions will be affected, according to business newsletter Feed Me, which first reported the news, layoffs have impacted the company’s creative team.

The layoffs came as an FDA grace period that allowed compounding pharmacies and outsourcing facilities to make copies of Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) weight loss therapy, semaglutide, ended this month after the agency declared an end to the U.S. shortage of the popular GLP-1 in February.

In May 2024, Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) introduced compounded versions of semaglutide at an 85% discount to Novo’s (NVO) product branded as Wegovy and Ozempic for weight loss and diabetes, respectively.  

“While not easy, this step reflects our commitment to invest in the areas that will define our future,” a spokesperson for the company said. “As part of this broader change, we will continue to actively hire for roles critical to our long-term strategy.”

data.title: Nvidia, Oracle could see huge revenue boosts from Stargate data center in Texas: UBS
data.description:
January's announcement of the artificial intelligence-focused Stargate Project boosted enthusiasm for the technology sector, amid the size and scope of the $500B project.

And while working is being done on the first data center in Abilene, TX. (with other sites to come), investment firm UBS has begun to quantify how much of a boost key players such as Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), OpenAI and others could get from the initiative.

Nvidia is likely to be the prime beneficiary of Stargate, UBS analysts Karl Keirstead, Timothy Arcuri and Radi Sultan wrote, particularly as OpenAI scales its compute needs via Oracle, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV).

At full capacity, the data center in Abilene implies a demand of 400,000 Nvidia GB200s, which Keirstead said could be worth roughly $20B in revenue, depending upon the GPU mix. “We also see additional networking revenue that could push the total opportunity to $25B,” the analysts wrote in a note to clients.

The revenue opportunity for Oracle is much lower than it is for Nvidia, but still significant nonetheless. Once phase 1 of Abilene is complete — construction on phase 2 has already started, nonetheless — it could provide a GPU cluster of 100,000 or more, the analysts added.

That could generate $2B or more in revenue for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, which the analysts said is “underappreciated” by investors. Additionally, the OpenAI compute spend could start in Oracle's fiscal second-quarter or the third-quarter by the latest, with the potential for a “material backlog event much sooner.”

For OpenAI, the opportunity is significant, especially given that it wants to own more of its infrastructure intellectual property and move down the tech stack, the analysts said.

“OpenAI’s growth and hence GPU compute needs are so material that OpenAI needs more AI infrastructure capacity than Microsoft was able (or in our view, willing) to provide, and as OpenAI’s CEO has said, the GPU capacity constraints became evident in [March and April] of this year and forced OpenAI to throttle back on other initiatives to serve a surge in ChatGPT demand,” the analysts wrote. “Additional capacity via the Abilene data center complex will allow OpenAI to move faster on its product roadmap.”
